title: Compare Dates in an If Statement (Calculating offset quarter)
excerpt: I’d like to use an If Statement in a Formula field to check if a date falls within a quarter.  Based on the range, I would note Q1, Q2, etc.  Is this possible?  I haven’t been able to compare a date yet.  May not be poss…

posts.author: BruceB (Bruce Baldwin)
posts.cookedHtml: I’d like to use an If Statement in a Formula field to check if a date falls within a quarter. Based on the range, I would note Q1, Q2, etc. Is this possible? I haven’t been able to compare a date yet. May not be possible or I just can’t find the correct syntax. It does look like I can trim to capture the month, so that may work. Ideally I could say IF({Request Date}>=1/1/2021 and <= 3/31/2021,“Q1”, Next check)

posts.author: timlittletech (Tim Little)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hey @BruceB

I think you are on the right track with calculating the month and determining if that falls in the correct range. I created 2 formula columns

  1. calculates month of the date Formula: MONTH({Date 2})
  2. takes month and calculates the Quarter that month is in. Formula: IF({Month}<=3,"Q1",IF(AND({Month}>3,{Month}<=6),"Q2",IF(AND({Month}>6,{Month}<=9),"Q3","Q4")))

This seemed to work for me. Although if you have blank dates, then I guess you’d have to add a 4th IF statement for Q4, I was just lazy and said if its not any of the other Qs then it must be Q4.

Another option is to create a bar chart of your date column, and group by Quarter. That is much simpler, but you also lose the ability to see which exact items are in which quarter.

Screen Shot 2021-06-30 at 8.35.29 AM2860×1524 207 KB

Hope that helps!

posts.author: JCorrell (Jim - The Monday Man)
posts.cookedHtml:
@BruceB,

Another alternative will return the quarter directly:

FORMAT_DATE({Date},"Q")

If you want the “Q” in front:

IF(FORMAT_DATE({Date},"Q")<>"","Q","") & FORMAT_DATE({Date},"Q")

posts.author: BruceB (Bruce Baldwin)
posts.cookedHtml:
JCorrell:

IF(FORMAT_DATE({Date},"Q")<>"","Q","") & FORMAT_DATE({Date},"Q")

Thanks. That’s a bit cleaner. It looks like it determines Q base on calendar year. What if you want to use your company’s fiscal year? Ours begins on Nov 1.

posts.author: JCorrell (Jim - The Monday Man)
posts.cookedHtml:
@BruceB,

That’s a little more complicated…

If you are sure that it will never be blank:

"Q" & ROUNDDOWN( DIVIDE( MOD( MONTH({Date})+1, 12), 3)+1, 0)

Otherwise:

IF({Date}<>"", "Q" & ROUNDDOWN( DIVIDE( MOD( MONTH(IF({Date}<>"",{Date},1))+1,12),3)+1,0),"")

posts.author: JCorrell (Jim - The Monday Man)
posts.cookedHtml:
@BruceB,

To answer your original question… probably the simplest way to compare date column values to a constant, put the constants in quotes and YYYY-MM-DD format. Like this:

IF({Date}<"2021-01-15", "Date is Before 1/15/2021", "Date is on or after 1/15/2021")

Otherwise, please remember to mark the answer as the “solution” to close out the topic and help future readers find the answer quickly!

Thanks,
Jenna

posts.author: Fab_PM (Erika)
posts.cookedHtml:
Hi! This formula is almost exactly what I need for my board, however, our fiscal year begins in July. Could someone assist with the correct formula to use? And maybe a small breakdown on how you came to the conclusion - been at it for about 4+ hours tryna crack the code! Thanks

Running into the issue of Q4 stating Q0 and I am using a due date column to reference the timing, would a timeline be easier in this case! Please help, my brain is fried at this point!
